text: The surgeon utilizes four laparoscopic ports: two 10mm ports for the camera and instruments, one 12mm port for potential stapler use, and a 5mm port for liver retraction during the adrenalectomy procedure.

The video displays the port positions for the laparoscopic right adrenalectomy, with two ten mm ports for the camera and instrument, one twelve mm port for potential stapler use, and a five mm port for liver retraction.
The laparoscopic camera port is positioned in the paramedian area, four fingerbreadths lateral to the oblique muscles, providing a view for the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon places working ports on either side of the surgical field, along with a sublipoid port specifically for liver retraction.
The surgeon begins dissection by releasing the liver attachments, revealing the kidney and adrenal gland in the supracolic compartment.
The surgeon retracts the liver using the subcephoid port after freeing its attachments, providing a clear view of the kidney and adrenal gland in the supracolic compartment.
After completing the previous steps, the adrenal tumor becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to divide the peripheral attachments of the adrenal gland, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully handles the adrenal gland, minimizing manipulation to prevent blood pressure surges, characteristic of pheochromocytoma cases.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to divide the liver attachments of the adrenal tumor and gland, carefully separating these structures from the surrounding tissue.
The adrenal gland and tumor are visible without any retraction or traction applied, maintaining a stable surgical field.
The surgeon initiates medial dissection of the adrenal gland, following the division of peripheral attachments using a hook cautery.
The surgeon excises the duodenum attachments, revealing the underlying inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to divide the delicate tissue connections between the adrenal gland and the inferior vena cava (IVC), carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the inferior vena cava (IVC) to prevent injury during the adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies mild traction to the adrenal gland, coordinating with the anesthetist to prevent blood pressure surges during the procedure.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect the adrenal vein, which is now visible in the surgical field, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects the adrenal vein, ensuring precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects the right-sided adrenal vein, which appears shorter and stockier, ensuring precision to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
Following the dissection, the surgeon applies two clips to the adrenal vein on the patient's side to secure it.
The surgeon applies a single clip on the specimen side of the adrenal vein and then divides the vein, completing the dissection.
The surgeon identifies an accessory adrenal vein on the right side, in addition to the usual single adrenal vein, and prepares to dissect it.
The surgeon carefully dissects the adrenal vein, applies clips to secure it, and then divides the vein, completing the separation process.
The surgeon applies double clips to the adrenal artery on the patient's side and divides it using a ligature on the specimen side, adjacent to the previously dissected adrenal vein.
After the vessel is divided, the adrenal gland is visibly lifted and freed from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues dissection in the established plane, progressively lifting the adrenal gland after dividing its attachments.
The surgeon applies traction to the adrenal gland after dividing the vein, facilitating its mobilization from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to divide the fibro fatty tissues surrounding the adrenal gland.
The surgeon divides the lower attachments of the perinephric fat using a ligature device, separating these tissues from the adrenal gland.
The surgeon moves from the medial aspect to the lateral aspect, joining the previously completed lateral dissection, facilitating the progressive lifting of the adrenal gland.
The surgeon carefully removes all visible adrenal tissue, ensuring complete excision from the surrounding area.
Following the completion of the dissection, the surgical field is irrigated with saline to identify any small bleeders or areas of oozing within the exposed tissue.
The surgeon uses electrocautery and ligature to ligate or cauterize the visible vessels and tissues in the surgical field, ensuring hemostasis and preventing any bleeding.
The surgeon places a surgical drain through the lateral port, ensuring proper drainage from the surgical site.
The surgeon places the adrenalectomy specimen into an endo bag for removal from the surgical site.
The surgeon extends the camera port slightly and removes the specimen from the port, completing the extraction process.
The video shows a post-operative view of the surgical site following the completion of the adrenalectomy procedure.
